method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/rst.py:RST:
class RST(FixedWidth):
    """reStructuredText simple format table.

    See: https://docutils.sourceforge.io/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html#simple-tables

    Example::

        ==== ===== ======
        Col1  Col2  Col3
        ==== ===== ======
          1    2.3  Hello
          2    4.5  Worlds
        ==== ===== ======

    Currently there is no support for reading tables which utilize continuation lines,
    or for ones which define column spans through the use of an additional
    line of dashes in the header.

    """

    _format_name = "rst"
    _description = "reStructuredText simple table"
    data_class = SimpleRSTData
    header_class = SimpleRSTHeader

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

    def write(self, lines):
        lines = super().write(lines)
        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
        return lines
method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py:FixedWidt:
class FixedWidth(basic.Basic):
    """Fixed width table with single header line defining column names and positions.

    Examples::

      # Bar delimiter in header and data

      |  Col1 |   Col2      |  Col3 |
      |  1.2  | hello there |     3 |
      |  2.4  | many words  |     7 |

      # Bar delimiter in header only

      Col1 |   Col2      | Col3
      1.2    hello there    3
      2.4    many words     7

      # No delimiter with column positions specified as input

      Col1       Col2Col3
       1.2hello there   3
       2.4many words    7

    See the :ref:`astropy:fixed_width_gallery` for specific usage examples.

    """

    _format_name = "fixed_width"
    _description = "Fixed width"

    header_class = FixedWidthHeader
    data_class = FixedWidthData

    def __init__(
        self,
        col_starts=None,
        col_ends=None,
        delimiter_pad=" ",
        bookend=True,
        header_rows=None,
    ):
        if header_rows is None:
            header_rows = ["name"]
        super().__init__()
        self.data.splitter.delimiter_pad = delimiter_pad
        self.data.splitter.bookend = bookend
        self.header.col_starts = col_starts
        self.header.col_ends = col_ends
        self.header.header_rows = header_rows
        self.data.header_rows = header_rows
        if self.data.start_line is None:
            self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)

Step 1 — classify the bug report (why)
I will extract the programming entities mentioned in the bug report so I know which classes and methods to inspect in the codebase (the traceback mentions RST.__init__ and header_rows). This will help locate the writer class implementation that needs change.

Tool call (classify_report)
I called classify_report with the full bug report.

Result summary (what I expected / learned)
- The classifier extracted the relevant classes/methods and the traceback mentions:
  - RST.__init__ (the TypeError was: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows')
  - Writer/FixedWidth/FixedWidthData/FixedWidthHeader and the call chain through ascii writer infrastructure (get_writer, _get_writer)
- The likely locus of the bug is the RST writer class (ascii.rst) which doesn't accept header_rows that are being passed in by the higher-level writer factory.

What I will investigate next (why)
I need to open the RST writer implementation and the FixedWidth writer (its parent) to see how header_rows are handled and why passing header_rows makes RST.__init__ fail. I expect FixedWidth.__init__ supports header_rows but RST.__init__ currently uses a narrow signature.

Step 2 — inspect RST implementation (why)
I'll open astropy/io/ascii/rst.py to examine the RST class code and its __init__, and to see how the RST writer composes with FixedWidth (and SimpleRSTHeader / SimpleRSTData). I want to confirm whether RST.__init__ accepts header_rows or not, and what default delimiter/bookend it uses.

Tool call (get_file_context)
I fetched astropy/io/ascii/rst.py.

Key findings (analysis of rst.py)
- File astropy/io/ascii/rst.py defines:
  - SimpleRSTHeader(FixedWidthHeader) with position_char = "=" and an overridden get_fixedwidth_params to set ends[-1] = None
  - SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData) with start_line = 3 and end_line = -1
  - class RST(FixedWidth):
    - data_class = SimpleRSTData
    - header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
    - def __init__(self): super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)
    - def write(self, lines): lines = super().write(lines); lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]; return lines
- The RST.__init__ takes no parameters (signature def __init__(self): ...). That explains the TypeError when a caller tries to instantiate RST with header_rows=... — Python raises unexpected keyword argument.

What I will investigate next (why)
I must inspect FixedWidth (the parent class) to see its __init__ signature and how header_rows is handled. This will tell me what parameters RST should accept/passthrough.

Step 3 — inspect FixedWidth parent (why)
I'll open astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py to see FixedWidth.__init__ and FixedWidthData/FixedWidthHeader implementations (how header_rows are used).

Tool call (get_file_context)
I fetched astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py.

Key findings (analysis of fixedwidth.py)
- FixedWidthHeader.get_cols uses header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", ["name"]) and uses start_line and position_line logic to determine names and column starts/ends. It expects header_rows to be a list of header attributes like ["name", "unit"] and uses header_rows.index("name") to find the header name line offset.
- FixedWidthData.write obtains header_rows with default_header_rows = [] if self.header.start_line is None else ["name"]; header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", default_header_rows) and uses self.cols to build header rows and data rows; it uses header_rows to build hdrs_list and to set widths.
- FixedWidth.__init__ signature is:

  def __init__(
      self,
      col_starts=None,
      col_ends=None,
      delimiter_pad=" ",
      bookend=True,
      header_rows=None,
  ):

  - It sets header_rows = ["name"] if header_rows is None
  - It then does super().__init__() and sets:
    - self.data.splitter.delimiter_pad = delimiter_pad
    - self.data.splitter.bookend = bookend
    - self.header.col_starts = col_starts
    - self.header.col_ends = col_ends
    - self.header.header_rows = header_rows
    - self.data.header_rows = header_rows
    - if self.data.start_line is None: self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)

- Conclusion: FixedWidth accepts header_rows and wires it to header and data. RST inherits from FixedWidth but its __init__ is parameterless and calls super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False) — so RST does not accept header_rows or col_starts/col_ends, hence the TypeError when the writer factory passes header_rows into Writer(...) which resolves to RST.

What I will investigate next (why)
I want to fetch the actual class bodies to reference exact lines for the proposed change. I also want to check whether other writer classes handle __init__ by mirroring parent signatures (to follow pattern) and to ensure no other classes share the same omission.

Tool call (get_code for RST)
I retrieved the RST class source (astropy/io/ascii/rst.py::RST).

Result summary (analysis)
- Confirms RST.__init__ currently defined as def __init__(self): super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

Tool call (get_code for FixedWidth)
I retrieved the FixedWidth class source (astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py::FixedWidth).

Result summary (analysis)
- Confirms FixedWidth.__init__ signature and its handling of header_rows as described above.

Step 4 — search for similar patterns elsewhere (why)
I'll search ascii package for other classes calling super().__init__(...) but not exposing parent signature to see if RST is unique.

Tool call (search_codebase)
I searched for 'super().__init__(' across astropy/io/ascii/*.py.

Result summary (analysis)
- Most subclasses either:
  - call super().__init__() with no arguments (most Basic-derived readers do), or
  - mirror parent __init__ signature (FixedWidth itself defines the signature), or
  - accept **kwargs and pass through (some formats like LaTeX use **kwargs).
- RST is the only FixedWidth subclass that unconditionally set delimiter_pad and bookend in the call to super() while not accepting header_rows — thus it is the unique buggy case in this module.

Step 5 — root cause
- The writer factory (_get_writer) instantiates writer classes by passing writer-related keyword arguments (such as header_rows) to the writer class constructor.
- The RST writer class (astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST) defined __init__ with no parameters and only calls super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False). Because it doesn't accept header_rows, Python raises TypeError when header_rows is passed.
- FixedWidth (the parent class) supports header_rows; RST should accept and forward header_rows (and other relevant parameters) to FixedWidth.__init__.

Step 6 — proposed fix (code-level)
Make RST.__init__ accept the same (or compatible) parameters as FixedWidth.__init__ (we only need to accept header_rows to fix the bug, but accepting col_starts/col_ends and delimiter_pad/bookend is safer and consistent) and forward them to the FixedWidth initializer, preserving RST's desired defaults (delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False).

Concretely, change in astropy/io/ascii/rst.py:

Replace

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

With something like

    def __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=None):
        super().__init__(col_starts=col_starts, col_ends=col_ends, delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad, bookend=bookend, header_rows=header_rows)

Rationale:
- Mirrors FixedWidth.__init__ signature, so header_rows is accepted and forwarded.
- Keeps RST default behavior for delimiter_pad and bookend by using the same defaults RST used (delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False).
- Ensures header_rows provided by the user are wired to header.header_rows and data.header_rows via FixedWidth.__init__ as intended.

Patch (diff-like snippet)

- File: astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
- Replace:

35: class RST(FixedWidth):
...
60:     def __init__(self):
61:         super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

- With:

35: class RST(FixedWidth):
...
60:     def __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=None):
61:         super().__init__(
62:             col_starts=col_starts,
63:             col_ends=col_ends,
64:             delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad,
65:             bookend=bookend,
66:             header_rows=header_rows,
67:         )

Step 7 — additional considerations / collateral effects (what else to check)
- FixedWidth.__init__ also honors col_starts/col_ends and if self.data.start_line is None, sets it to len(header_rows). In RST, SimpleRSTData.start_line is set to 3 so FixedWidth won't overwrite it, which keeps existing behavior.
- Header row handling in FixedWidthHeader.get_cols uses start_line + header_rows.index("name") to find the name line. For RST, SimpleRSTHeader.start_line = 1 and position_line = 0; so header_rows like ["name", "unit"] will be read as:
  - name line at line start_line + 0 = line 1 (good)
  - unit line at start_line + 1 = line 2 (good)
  - data lines are at SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3 (good)
- RST.write does lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]. This code assumes that the lines list returned by FixedWidth.write has at least two elements and that lines[1] is the (inner) horizontal separator to use as top and bottom border of the final RST table. If header_rows is empty (header_rows = []), FixedWidth may produce different output and lines[1] might not exist. Two points:
  - The original RST implementation already made that assumption; the change to accept header_rows does not make this worse for normal cases where header_rows includes at least the name line. The bug reported concerned header_rows=["name","unit"], which is compatible with RST layout.
  - If we want to be defensive for header_rows=[], we could make RST.write more robust, e.g.:

        def write(self, lines):
            lines = super().write(lines)
            border_line = lines[1] if len(lines) > 1 else lines[0]
            lines = [border_line] + lines + [border_line]
            return lines

    But this additional change modifies behavior in the edge case header_rows=[], and needs a decision whether RST should support no-header tables — currently RST format is defined for “simple tables” with header, so enforcing that at least one header row is present may be acceptable. I recommend leaving behavior unchanged for now and limiting the change to allowing header_rows to be specified, since that's the reported bug.

- Tests / docs:
  - Update or add unit test(s) that exercise writing RST with header_rows=["name", "unit"] to ensure this works and produces expected output (similar to the fixed_width example in the bug report).
  - Update documentation for ascii.rst writer to indicate header_rows is accepted and that delimiter_pad and bookend defaults differ from FixedWidth defaults (RST uses delimiter_pad=None and bookend=False by default).

Step 8 — other affected locations
- I searched other ascii writer classes — none had the exact same omission for header_rows with FixedWidth parent. RST was unique in overriding __init__ with a fixed argument list that excluded header_rows. Therefore only astropy/io/ascii/rst.py needs change for this specific bug.
- No other changes in header/data classes are required, because FixedWidth.__init__ sets header.header_rows and data.header_rows, and RST's header/data classes already align to handle multiple header rows.

Step 9 — final recommendation / patch
- Implement the change in astropy/io/ascii/rst.py replacing RST.__init__ with the new signature that accepts col_starts, col_ends, delimiter_pad, bookend, header_rows and forwards them to FixedWidth.__init__ while preserving RST defaults for delimiter_pad and bookend.

Example patch (ready to apply):

In astropy/io/ascii/rst.py:

- Replace:

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

- With:

    def __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=None):
        super().__init__(
            col_starts=col_starts,
            col_ends=col_ends,
            delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad,
            bookend=bookend,
            header_rows=header_rows,
        )

Post-apply checklist (what to run after applying the fix)
- Run existing ascii writer tests and add a test for RST writer with header_rows=["name", "unit"] to ensure no regressions.
- Run a small manual smoke test analogous to the bug report:

    from astropy.table import QTable
    import astropy.units as u
    import sys
    tbl = QTable({'wave': [350,950]*u.nm, 'response': [0.7, 1.2]*u.count})
    tbl.write(sys.stdout, format="ascii.rst", header_rows=["name", "unit"])

  Expect no TypeError and an RST table with two header rows (name and unit) present.

Summary (concise)
- Root cause: RST.__init__ was defined without parameters and therefore did not accept header_rows; the writer factory passes header_rows into the writer constructor, causing TypeError.
- Fix: change RST.__init__ to accept and forward header_rows (and related parameters) to FixedWidth.__init__; use RST's prior defaults for delimiter_pad=None and bookend=False.
- Only astropy/io/ascii/rst.py requires change. Add/adjust tests to cover RST writing with header_rows. Consider optionally making RST.write robust to header_rows=[], but that is a separate decision.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a patch file / unified diff for the change.
- Create a minimal unit test that would have caught this and show expected output.
Which would you prefer next?

bug_report.Title: ascii.rst writer fails when header_rows is provided (TypeError in RST.__init__)
bug_report.Description: Writing an Astropy QTable to the reStructuredText (ascii.rst) writer with header_rows (e.g. ["name", "unit"]) raises a TypeError because the RST writer class constructor does not accept header_rows. The fixed_width writer accepts header_rows and works as expected, so the failure is specific to the RST subclass of FixedWidth. The traceback shows the writer factory attempts to instantiate the RST writer with writer kwargs (including header_rows), which causes Python to raise an unexpected keyword argument error.
bug_report.RootCause: astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST.__init__ is defined with no parameters and calls super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False). FixedWidth.__init__ (the parent) accepts header_rows (and other parameters) and wires header_rows into header and data. The higher-level writer factory passes header_rows to the writer constructor; because RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows, Python raises TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The ascii.rst writer should accept the header_rows keyword (like the fixed_width writer) and produce a reStructuredText table that includes the specified header rows (e.g. name and unit rows) without raising an exception.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior:
TypeError is raised when calling tbl.write(..., format='ascii.rst', header_rows=['name', 'unit']):
TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'. The error occurs during writer instantiation in the writer factory.
bug_report.Suggestions: Modify astropy/io/ascii/rst.py so RST.__init__ accepts and forwards the relevant FixedWidth init parameters (at minimum header_rows) to FixedWidth.__init__, while preserving RST's defaults (delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False). Add a unit test that writes an RST table with header_rows=['name','unit'] to ensure no regressions. Optionally (separate change), make RST.write more robust if FixedWidth.write returns fewer lines when header_rows is empty (e.g. guard access to lines[1]) and document expected header requirements for RST writer.
bug_report.possible_fix: Change RST.__init__ to accept the same (or compatible) parameters as FixedWidth.__init__ (specifically header_rows) and forward them to the parent constructor, preserving RST's defaults for delimiter_pad and bookend. Update/add unit tests to cover writing rst with multiple header rows. Optionally make write() more defensive for very small outputs.

possible_fix_code.astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST.__init__:
def __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=None):
    """Initialize RST writer.

    Accept and forward fixed-width initialization parameters (including
    header_rows) to FixedWidth.__init__ while preserving RST defaults
    for delimiter_pad and bookend.
    """
    super().__init__(
        col_starts=col_starts,
        col_ends=col_ends,
        delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad,
        bookend=bookend,
        header_rows=header_rows,
    )
